slides.title: Computing AlgorithmsLecture 10:Transform and Conquer
content: By: Dr. Mohamed Moheeb

slides.title: Agenda
content: Introduction
Presorting
Balanced search tree
Heap sort
That are based on the idea of transformation. We call this general technique transform-and-conquer because these methods work as two-stage procedures.
The transformation stage, the problem’s instance is modified to be, for one reason or another, more amenable to solution.
The conquering stage, it is solved

slides.title: Presorting
content: Presorting is an old idea in computer science.
In fact, interest in sorting algorithms is due, to a significant degree, to the fact that many questions about a list are easier to answer if the list is sorted.
The time efficiency of algorithms that involve sorting may depend on the efficiency of the sorting algorithm being used.

slides.title: Checking element uniqueness in an array
Checking element uniqueness in an array
Binary search trees
content: Binary search tree (BTS) is a tree with at most two children nodes for each parent node
For any given node, all values in its left subtree must be smaller, and all values in its right subtree must be larger
Why use tree not just arrays or linked lists?
Arrays: While a sorted array allows for O(log n) search, inserting or deleting an element is slow (O(n)) because you have to shift all other elements.
Linked Lists: These allow for fast insertion once you find the spot, but searching is always O(n) because you have to traverse them linearly.
BST Advantage: BST aims to provide the "best of both worlds": efficient searching and efficient dynamic updates (insertions/deletions).

slides.title: Balanced Binary Search Tree
content: In a randomly built tree, the height is logarithmic, making search, insertion, and deletion very efficient  θ (log(n)))
But if the elements are inserted in ascending or descending order the time complexity is  θ (n)
How to solve this problem ? by making creating a balanced binary tree
A Balanced (BTS) is method used to ensure the height of the tree doesn’t exceed a certain height on both sides
There is two types of algorithm:
AVL Tree
2-3 Tree

slides.title: AVL tree
content: An AVL Tree (named after its inventors Adelson- Velsky  and Landis) is a self-balancing Binary Search Tree (BST).
It was the first data structure of its kind to be developed, specifically designed to address the "skewing" problem where a standard BST can become inefficient and act like a linked list.

The defining characteristic of an AVL tree is that it maintains a strict balance during every insertion and deletion. This ensures that the tree height remains logarithmic relative to the number of nodes, guaranteeing that operations like search, insertion, and deletion  ϴ (log(n))

slides.title: AVL Tree
content: an AVL tree is the Balance Factor (BF). For every node in the tree, the balance factor is calculated
Balance Factor = Height of Left Subtree – Height of Right Subtree
To remain "balanced," an AVL tree enforces a strict rule: the balance factor of every node must be either -1, 0, or +1.
If the BF of any node becomes +2 or -2 after an update, the tree is considered "unbalanced.“
The tree must then perform a rotation to restore its balance.
To restore balance without breaking the BST property (Left < Parent < Right), the tree performs one of four  rotations.

slides.title: AVL Example
content: Construction of an AVL tree for the list 5, 6, 8, 3, 2, 4, 7 by successive insertions.

slides.title: AVL Example
content: Construction of an AVL tree for the list 5, 6, 8, 3, 2, 4, 7 by successive insertions.

slides.title: 2-3 tree
content: It is also used to balance a search tree is to allow more than one key in the same node of such a tree
A 2-3 tree is a tree that can have nodes of two kinds:2-nodes and 3-nodes.
A 2-node contains a single key K and has two children:
the left child serves as the root of a subtree whose keys are less than K
the right child serves as the root of a subtree whose keys are greater than K.
In other words, a 2-node is the same kind of node we have in the classical binary search tree.)

slides.title: 2-3 tree
content: It is also used to balance a search tree is to allow more than one key in the same node of such a tree
A 2-3 tree is a tree that can have nodes of two kinds:2-nodes and 3-nodes.
A 2-node contains a single key K and has two children:
the left child serves as the root of a subtree whose keys are less than K
the right child serves as the root of a subtree whose keys are greater than K.
In other words, a 2-node is the same kind of node we have in the classical binary search tree.)

slides.title: 2-3 tree
content: A 3-node contains two ordered keys K1 and K2 (K1 < K2) and has three children.
The leftmost child serves as the root of a subtree with keys less than K1,
the middle child serves as the root of a subtree with keys between K1 and K2,
the rightmost child serves as the root of a subtree with keys greater than K2

slides.title: 2-3 tree Example
content: Construction of a 2-3 tree for the list 9, 5, 8, 3, 2, 4, 7

slides.title: Heap
content: A heap can be defined as a binary tree with keys assigned to its nodes, one key per node, provided the following two conditions are met:
The shape property—the binary tree is essentially complete (or simply complete), i.e., all its levels are full except possibly the last level, where only some rightmost leaves may be missing.
The parental dominance or heap property—the key in each node is greater than or equal to the keys in its children.

slides.title: Important properties of heaps
content: There exists exactly one essentially complete binary tree with n nodes. Its height is equal to [log2 n].
The root of a heap always contains its largest element.
A node of a heap considered with all its descendants is also a heap.

slides.title: Important properties of heaps
content: 4. A heap can be implemented as an array by recording its elements in the top-down, left-to-right fashion. It is convenient to store the heap's elements in positions 1 through n of such an array, leaving H[0] either unused or putting there a value that is greater than every element in the heap:
the parental node keys will be in the first [n/2] positions of the array, while the leaf keys will occupy the last [n/2] positions.

the children of a key in the array's parental position  i  (1 ≤  i  ≤ [n/2]) will be in positions 2i and 2i + 1, and, correspondingly, the parent of a key in position  i  (2 ≤  i  ≤ n) will be in position [ i /2].

slides.title: Heap-bottom up
content: Let’s trace on this list 2, 9, 7, 6, 5, 8 using array this time

slides.title: Top-down heap construction
content: Initialize : Start with an empty heap.
Insert : For each element in the input set:
Place the element at the first available leaf position (the end of the heap array) to maintain the complete binary tree property.
Up- Heapify  (Sift-Up) : Compare the newly added element with its parent. If it violates the heap property (e.g., it is larger than its parent in a max-heap), swap them.
Continue swapping the element with its parent until it reaches the root or its correct position where the heap property is satisfied.
Repeat : Repeat these steps until all input elements are processed
Time complexity O( nlogn )
This is because each of the insertions can take up to log(n) time as the element "bubbles up" the height of the tree.

slides.title: Top-down heap construction (successive key insertions)
content: Let’s trace on this list 2, 9, 7, 6, 5, 8 using the same array

slides.title: Maximum Key Deletion algorithm
content: Algorithm
Step 1 Exchange the root’s key with the last key K of the heap.
Step 2 Decrease the heap’s size by 1.

Step 3 “ Heapify ” the smaller tree by sifting K down the tree exactly in the same way we did it in the bottom-up heap construction algorithm. That is, verify the parental dominance for K: if it holds, we are done; if not, swap K with the larger of its children and repeat this operation until the parental dominance condition holds for K in its new position.

The efficiency of deletion is determined by the number of key comparisons needed to “ heapify ” the tree after the swap has been made and the size of the tree is decreased by 1. Since this cannot require more key comparisons than twice the heap’s height, the time efficiency of deletion is in O(log n)

slides.title: Heap sort
content: Stage 1 (heap construction): Construct a heap for a given array. (Heap-bottom up)
Stage 2 (maximum deletions): Apply the root-deletion operation n − 1 times to the remaining heap.

slides.title: Heap sort time complexity
content: Stage 1 :  As we just broke down in the previous math, we build the initial heap from an unordered array using the bottom-up approach. Time Complexity: O(n).
Stage 2: As we stated before to delete one element and  heapifiy  the list will take O(log(n)) and since we are going to  heapifiy  the list n times then the total complexity is O( nlog (n))
By using both stages the final output is time complexity for heap sort is O( nlog (n))
